author: @billschwandt1
text: I got a great idea for using your impossible gears. What's great about them, is they help one way, but they make it much harder the other, and you know what that's really good for? Flapping wings. You know those wing suits skydivers wear? We could reinforce the arms of one with your gears and they could easily fold their wings in and dive, but then when they want to fling their arms out and give resistance so they don't ever have too much force exerted on them in weird ways. This would also make for excellent exosuit tech for the military. 🫡

author: @gidi1899
text:
I love thinking about it,
similar to your words,
2 images that look the same - 2 rolling gears that touch.
2 different movements - one with 2 constant centers and one with one constant and one orbiting center.
2 cameras producing the same image - one constant above midway between centers and one above and orbiting and spining.

could you guess the stationary/constant points,
if given an overlayed image of the pressure flow (within some resolution)?!

author: @jonaslennstrom2752
text:
When i hear "impossible gears" I think of the intro to a tv show in Sweden, SVT, called "Tekniskt Magasin" from my childhood. I estimate its about 40 years ago.
The gears have different shape but still fit together. The odd shape is giving the result that a gear with fixed speed can give other gears variating speed.

author: @pathanrahan7578
text: Here is another way of thinking about it. Keep the center gear fixed and roll the outer gear around it. The outer gear will rotate one revolution as it rolls around the fixed gear. Now rotate the center gear as the outer gear rolls around it. Rotating the center gear once adds one more revolution to the outer gear. The outer gear rotates twice and the center gear once. This reasoning can be applied to different sized gears.

author: @GettinReal
text:
Thank you.  Been homeless so long I need a spot of engineering instruction to build my next project using a community center to help me that has all the tools I need and experienced tradesmen volunteers.  It's going to be an expandable trailer that is suitable for an ebike yet transformable to an ultra tiny home.

I just can't study Cybersecurity and other things on a laptop in an alleyway heh...

Hate tents, use paracord and a tarp as trained by an Aussie Commando in my twenties. Have a place to store it during the day when at my hopefully new job, or at job if close enough, and hopefully all will go to plan. 

 Making solid metal collapsable and extendable panels will be my greatest challenge but learning gears and the mechanics will be extremely important too to maximise battery efficiency.

Going to buy a bunch of powerbanks initially for power needs then go the mini wind turbine route and an inverter plus battery down the track.

Thank you for your channel.  I don't comment much but learn a lot from you and your commenters, Sir.

author: @jillianonthehudson1739
text: It took me a couple viewings to understand what, relativistically, was happening. At first I couldn't understand why a wheel following the outside of another was different so different - after all, there's no privileged POV - but then I realized that when both wheels' centers are fixed, then you're adding both circumferences together as both are spinning (n+1, where n=1), whereas when one wheel is fixed in place and the other is orbiting, only one circumference is moving. The positions aren't privileged, they just aren't equivalent

author: @WarblesWarkly
text:
Unrelated I know but, on a previous video I mentioned PA-6 or PA-12 (nylon) blends for gears.

If anyone wanted to know the basic pre and post processing methods for Nylon then CNC Kitchen (youtube) recently released a video for PA-6/PA-12 specifically.

I follow his method, except I do the annealing step in the vacuum chamber. I have a cheap pot with a lid and a vacuum pump. Pretty sure you've seen something similar here on yt.

I place the pot on a heating element intended for a kettle. Then i put the part in the pot on a little table. Turn on heating element and vacuum. I can only get the inside of the pot to 70°C.

80° is ideal- still on the low end though. The reason why you want it lower is because the steam being released may crack or warp the part during the process.

So low and slow works for me, but it may not work for you at first. Try adjusting temperature between 80-90°C and double check there are no leaks anywhere or that your pump is clogged or jammed.

I don't recommend going much higher than 90°C as you may fry your pump.
